Unit 5 — Criminal Case Process Lesson 1 of 4 Instructor Mrs. Henson
Lesson 5.1

Finding Justice

Case File Roper v. Simmons  |  543 U.S. 551 (2005)
Defendant
Christopher Simmons
Date of Crime
September 1993
Age at Time of Crime
17 years old
Jurisdiction
State of Missouri

In September 1993, Christopher Simmons, a seventeen-year-old from Missouri, broke into the home of Shirley Crook. Along with an accomplice, he bound and gagged Crook before placing her in a minivan. Simmons drove the woman to a state park near the Meramec River. On a train trestle over the river, the two teens tied up Crook's hands and feet and covered her head with a towel. They pushed Crook off the trestle and into the river below. She drowned, and her body was recovered the following day.


Christopher Simmons was arrested for the crime and sentenced to the death penalty. The case against him was strong; he had confessed to the crime and there was evidence that he had premeditated the murder.


However, the Supreme Court ruled during an appeal that no one under the age of eighteen (at the time when their crime was committed) should be executed in the United States. Simmons was resentenced to life in prison without the possibility of parole.

Discussion — Consider This

Do you think Simmons should have faced the death penalty? Under what circumstances should a juvenile be treated as an adult in the court system? This case illustrates two topics we will be considering in this unit: the criminal case process and juvenile justice.

I
A History of Inconsistent Justice

For much of history, punishing crime and deviance was a haphazard and inconsistent endeavor. A local community might have different laws and policies than the next town, and the punishments handed out were often dependent on who was doing the punishing rather than by standards of severity and type of punishment.

Some examples of early American justice can be found below:

Example 01
John Smith Becomes President of Jamestown Colony
The first colony in America was established with a ruling council of seven men, but in September of 1608, John Smith was elected as president. He immediately set forth laws for the settlers — one such being: if a man doesn't work, he doesn't eat.
Example 02
Puritans Conduct the Salem Witch Trials
Local magistrates did allow grand juries to decide whether to bring charges against those accused of witchcraft. Still, the trials and executions were held in less than a day due to mass hysteria on the part of the public.
Example 03
The San Francisco Vigilance Committee
The term "Wild West" was coined for a reason. During the Gold Rush in the 1850s, some San Francisco residents felt that local magistrates were corrupt. Citizens formed their own militia — whipping and deporting many without the permission of city officials.
Example 04
Plantation Justice in the Old South
Plantations were viewed as their own little nation, with the owner having enormous power that local and state government couldn't touch. The plantation owner exercised absolute power as both judge and executioner over enslaved people.
II
The Birth of a Systematic Justice Process

A systematic criminal justice process did not begin in the United States until 1929, when President Herbert Hoover created the Wickersham Commission — a federal body whose mandate was to improve justice system practices and to reinstate the law's role in civilized governance.

Key Term — Definition
Wickersham Report (1931)
A 1931 federal report that examined the American criminal justice system and concluded that there was little actual justice being served. It was produced by the Wickersham Commission, a group created by President Herbert Hoover in 1929 to study and improve justice system practices across the country.

In the years that followed, the laws and policies in the federal and state criminal justice systems began to be standardized as the Supreme Court began setting rules and regulations about police procedures and corrections.

III
The Criminal Justice System Today

The criminal justice system includes three interconnected parts: the police, the courts, and corrections. The various parts of the system are interrelated to each other and work together to form the whole system of criminal justice. The police, courts, and corrections all have common goals in regards to crime.
